data.title: Joby Aviation accuses rival Archer Aviation of corporate espionage
data.description:
Joby Aviation (JOBY) has filed a lawsuit against rival Archer Aviation (ACHR) in a California Superior Court in Santa Cruz, alleging that confidential information taken by a former employee who went to work for Archer (ACHR) was used to disrupt Joby’s exclusive client partnership.

According to court documents, Joby’s (JOBY) then U.S. state and local policy head, George Kivork, had access to confidential and proprietary information regarding Joby’s strategic partnerships, technical information, and sensitive information that would be valuable to a competitor.

Two days prior to announcing his resignation, Joby (JOBY) alleges Kivork exfiltrated a cache of “highly valuable Joby files.” Less than three weeks later, Joby (JOBY) was notified by a strategic partner that they were approached by Archer (ACHR) with detailed knowledge about the confidential terms of that partner’s agreement with Joby (JOBY).

“This is corporate espionage, planned and premeditated,” Joby states in its lawsuit.

In a statement to CNBC, Archer’s (ACHR) chief legal officer called Joby’s (JOBY) allegations “without merit,” and the complaint “does not identify a single specific trade secret, let alone any evidence of misappropriation.”

A hearing is scheduled for March 20, 2026.

data.title: Copa narrows 2025 operating margin guidance to 22–23% as fleet expands and demand remains robust
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Copa Holdings (CPA) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Pedro Heilbron highlighted Copa's industry-leading profitability in the third quarter, reporting "an operating margin of 23.2%, up 2.9 percentage points year-over-year and a net margin of 19%, up 1.9 percentage points year-over-year." He attributed the results to "continued focus on cost discipline and a healthy demand environment in the region."
  • Copa increased capacity in available seat miles (ASM) by 5.8% compared to the same period last year, with load factor rising to 88%. Passenger yields declined by 2.6% year-over-year, but unit revenues (RASM) increased 1% to $0.111. Unit costs (CASM) dropped 2.7% to $0.085, and CASM excluding fuel decreased 0.8% to $0.056.
  • Copa launched new flights to Salta and Tocumen in Argentina and anticipates adding service to Los Cabos, Mexico, Puerto Plata and Santiago in the Dominican Republic, and Salvador, Bahia in Brazil. The airline took delivery of five 737 MAX 8 aircraft and added a second Boeing 737-800 freighter under lease, closing the quarter with 121 aircraft and now operating 123.
  • Heilbron stated, "Going forward, our guidance demonstrates confidence in our future performance, driven by healthy demand in the region and the strength of our business model."
  • CFO Peter Donkersloot Ponce reported, "Net profit came in at $173 million or $4.20 per share compared to $146 million or $3.50 per share in the third quarter of 2024, representing a year-over-year increase of 18.7% and 20.1%, respectively." He also stated, "Operating income reached $212 million or 22.2% higher year-over-year, and an industry-leading operating margin of 23.2%."
  • Donkersloot Ponce added, "We ended the quarter with $1.3 billion in cash, short-term and long-term investments, representing 38% of the last 12-month revenues."

Outlook

  • Donkersloot Ponce reaffirmed full-year 2025 guidance and narrowed the operating margin range "to the upper end now expected between 22% and 23%, with a full year capacity growth projected at approximately 8%." The outlook is based on "a load factor of approximately 87%; RASM of approximately $0.112; ex-fuel CASM of approximately $0.058; and an all-in fuel price of $2.40 per gallon."
  • For 2026, Copa preliminarily expects "full year ASM capacity growth in the range between 11% to 13%, with an ex-fuel CASM in the range of $0.057 to $0.058."

Financial Results

  • Net profit for the quarter was $173 million or $4.20 per share, reflecting significant year-over-year growth. Operating income reached $212 million, and the company achieved an operating margin of 23.2%.
  • CASM decreased 2.7% year-over-year to $0.085, primarily due to lower fuel and maintenance expense. CASM excluding fuel was $0.056, helped by a realized gain from engine exchange transactions and a benefit related to the extension of one leased aircraft.
  • Copa distributed its fourth dividend of the year at $1.61 per share, to be paid on December 15.
  • The company closed the quarter with $1.3 billion in cash, $600 million in predelivery deposits, and 45 unencumbered aircraft. Total debt was $2.2 billion, all related to aircraft financing.

Q&A

  • Savanthi Syth, Raymond James & Associates: Asked about the co-branded credit card renewal and loyalty growth. Donkersloot Ponce responded that the Visa agreement renewal drove significant growth, with loyalty program growth "over 30% year-over-year over a small basis," and expects this to continue as the program matures.
  • Michael Linenberg, Deutsche Bank: Inquired about ASM growth and impact on unit revenues. Heilbron explained, "Most of the growth comes either full year effect or from adding frequencies...we're adding those frequencies in high-demand routes," suggesting less impact on unit revenues from capacity growth.
  • Jacob Gunning, Evercore ISI: Asked about 2026 margin outlook and capital allocation. Heilbron said expectations on unit cost and revenues "haven't really changed," noting fuel as the main uncertainty. Donkersloot Ponce reaffirmed priorities on reinvestment and shareholder returns, with dividends and an ongoing share buyback program.
  • Filipe Ferreira Nielsen, Citi: Asked about CASM ex guidance and future cost drivers. Donkersloot Ponce said the $0.058 guidance is a range and that engine exchange benefits are not necessarily one-offs, with cost initiatives in place to offset inflation in 2026.
  • Daniel McKenzie, Seaport Research Partners: Sought color on regional demand and growth durability. Heilbron described demand as healthy, with strong load factors and specific adjustments in Brazil and Argentina based on capacity and macro trends. For long-term growth, Copa expects "around 7%, 8% average growth CAGR" over four years, with flexibility to slow or adjust as needed.
  • Alberto Valerio, UBS: Asked about yields and competition. Heilbron did not provide quarterly yield guidance but noted the narrowed operating margin range. On competition, he stated Copa's focus on product, unit costs, and network strength, and commented on other airlines' IPOs and restructuring.
  • Thomas Fitzgerald, TD Cowen: Sought details on incremental frequency impact and technology investments. Donkersloot Ponce and Heilbron noted most growth comes from frequency increases, with new technology and merchandising initiatives aimed at enhancing ancillary revenues.
  • Guilherme Mendes, JPMorgan: Asked about competition and hedging. Heilbron discussed improvements in Brazil and Argentina, with stable or improving load factors. On fuel hedging, Heilbron said, "We're not planning to change our hedging strategy. We're happy with not hedging."
  • Syth, Raymond James: Inquired about seat densification. Donkersloot Ponce said about half of the planned densification is complete, with another 25 aircraft planned for 2026.
  • Jens Spiess, Morgan Stanley: Asked about guidance conservatism. Heilbron said, "We're very comfortable with that range between 22% and 23%."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on loyalty growth, cost assumptions, frequency impacts, competition, and macro demand, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they acknowledged strong results but probed for sustainability and conservatism in guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently expressing comfort with guidance and highlighting cost discipline and demand strength. Phrases like "we remain confident" and "we expect the program to continue to grow" underscored this confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more constructive on guidance and outlook, while analysts' tone shifted from cautious optimism to a more probing stance on growth and cost drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Copa raised its operating margin guidance to the upper end (22–23%) versus the previous quarter's 21–23% range, reflecting improved confidence and ongoing cost discipline.
  • Capacity growth projections for 2025 remain similar, but preliminary 2026 guidance now anticipates ASM growth of 11–13% and lower ex-fuel CASM.
  • Analysts' questions evolved from focusing on demand resilience and regional macro factors to more detailed inquiries about loyalty programs, cost structures, and competitive dynamics.
  • Management's confidence in sustaining industry-leading margins and growth initiatives remained consistent, with more specificity on fleet plans and ancillary revenue opportunities in the current quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited fuel price volatility as the primary wild card for future margins, with no plans to change the company's non-hedging strategy.
  • Competitive pressure in Argentina and Brazil was noted, with Copa adjusting capacity growth in response to regional developments.
  • Analysts probed for possible yield deceleration and cost increases, especially relating to seat densification and engine exchange transactions.

Final Takeaway

Copa delivered another strong quarter, underpinned by record operating and net margins, disciplined cost control, and robust cash reserves. Management reaffirmed and tightened full-year guidance, signaled continued fleet expansion, and emphasized strategic growth in both network and ancillary revenues. The outlook for 2026 points to further capacity increases and cost efficiency, with flexibility to adapt to market changes and a clear focus on maintaining industry-leading financial performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: UGI Non-GAAP EPS of $3.32 beats by $0.14, revenue of $7.29B misses by $120M
data.description:
• UGI press release (UGI): FY Non-GAAP EPS of $3.32 beats by $0.14.
  • Revenue of $7.29B (+1.1% Y/Y) misses by $120M.

  • 2026 OUTLOOK

    UGI provides an adjusted EPS guidance range of $2.90 - $3.152 per diluted share for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2026. This guidance range assumes normal weather and the current tax regime.

  • Shares -0.27% AH.

data.title: Gold slips after U.S. job growth defies expectations
data.description:
Gold prices finished lower for the fifth time in six sessions Thursday, following a U.S. employment report for September that was stronger than expected and appeared to dampen prospects for a December interest rate cut from the Federal Reserve.

Payrolls rose by 119,000 in September, well above the gain of 50,000 jobs expected by economists, while the unemployment rate rose slightly to 4.4%.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics has canceled the release of the October report and instead will combine the results with November's figures to be released on December 16, after the Fed's next meeting, adding to scaled back expectations for a quarter-point rate cut at that time.

The jobs data "essentially confirms what the Fed discussed in October - a slowing yet stable jobs market. A December rate cut now appears increasingly unlikely," adding pressure to gold, Xaner Metals senior metals strategist Peter Grant said in a note.

Traders are now pricing in just a 42% chance of a December rate cut, according to the FedWatch tool.

Despite the recent gold price consolidation, UBS raised its 2026 mid-year gold target price by $300/oz to $4,500/oz, citing Fed rate cut expectations, persistent geopolitical risks, fiscal concerns and strong demand from central banks and ETF investors, as well as its upside target by $200 to $4,900/oz, on a possible spike of political and financial risks.

"The dollar and Treasury yields strengthened following the latest FOMC minutes, which signaled limited appetite for a December rate cut," Saxo Bank analysts said in a note, but "bullion remains in consolidation mode after rising more than 50% this year, supported by a broad mix of structural and macro drivers ahead of 2026."

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for November delivery closed -0.5% to $4,056.50/oz, its lowest settlement since November 7, and front-month Comex November silver (XAGUSD:CUR) ended -1.1% to $50.247/oz, its lowest settlement since November 10.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Wall Street's slide likely driven by signals in crypto - Fundstrat's Lee, IBKR's Sosnick
data.description:
Wall Street on Thursday experienced a stunning reversal in sentiment. Blowout results from AI darling Nvidia (NVDA) and a strong September jobs report initially sent the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) up more than 2%. By noon, all those gains had disappeared, and by the close, the tech-heavy index had slid more than 2%.

The turnaround was due to several reasons, such as a historical pattern of sharp intraday swings in Nvidia (NVDA) stock and a hit to interest rate cut expectations from a more mixed jobs report than initially thought. However, according to Fundstrat's head of research, Tom Lee, and Interactive Brokers' chief strategist, Steve Sosnick, developments in the crypto space were the possible triggers.

"To me it was kind of uncanny. I was on the phone with someone discussing how good the market was doing, and I said, 'Bitcoin is starting here to flirt with $90K; if it breaks, I have a feeling that the S&Ps can follow.' And it was uncanny; as soon as we broke $90K, down went the Nasdaq and the S&P 500," Sosnick told CNBC. 

"I'm not going to say that bitcoin is the reason that we had the big intraday reversal. Bitcoin was the proximate trigger, because I do think right now algorithmic traders are using bitcoin as what we would call a lead. Right now it's being used as a proxy for speculative fever. If I'm noticing it, and you're noticing it, algorithms are noticing it. And so money is being allocated on this, rationally or not," he added.

The world's largest cryptocurrency was last down -3.9% at $87,930.40. It has cratered more than 30% from its record high amid an overall recent rout in the crypto world that has hit investors both big and small. According to media reports, a series of liquidations on October 10 that saw the offloading of more than $19B of leveraged crypto positions triggered the most recent move lower.

"I'm going to kind of add to what Steve (Sosnick) said. I think the crypto market has been limping along since October 10 because on that date was a negative shock. I mean, today's stock market looks a lot like an echo of what happened on October 10," Fundstrat's Lee told CNBC. 

Wall Street had suffered heavy selling on October 10, with the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) falling -2.7%. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) had slumped -7.2%.   

"But on October 10, that liquidation was so big...it really crippled market makers. And our market makers are critical in crypto because they provide liquidity. I mean, they act almost as the central bank in crypto. And if they've got a hole in their balance sheet that they need to raise capital for, they need to reflexively reduce their balance sheet, reduce trading, and if prices fall, they've got to then do more selling," Lee explained. 

"So I think that this drip that's been taking place for the last few weeks in crypto reflects this market maker crippling, and so in 2022 it took eight weeks for that to really get flushed out. We're only six weeks into it, so I kind of concur. I think crypto, bitcoin, and ethereum (ETH-USD) are in some ways a leading indicator for equities because of that unwind and now this sort of limping and weakened liquidity," he added. 

After Thursday's losses, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) and the S&P 500 (SP500) are on track for their worst week since the start of April. Here are some exchange-traded funds that track both indexes: (QQQ), (QLD), (TQQQ), (QID), (SQQQ), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

And here are some crypto ETFs: (IBIT), (ARKB), (GBTC), (BRRR), (BTCO), (HODL), (BTCW), (FBTC), (BITB), (EZBC), (BITQ), (DAPP), (BKCH), (BLOK), (CRPT), and (IBLC).

data.title: Kroger hits the breaks on costly automation in bid for more profitable digital grocery business
data.description:
Kroger’s (KR) bet on automation didn’t deliver the efficiencies and cost savings the company was betting on and led to the decision to close three of its automated warehouses and shift the bulk of its grocery delivery to third-party vendors.

Earlier this week, Kroger (KR) announced an update to its e-commerce strategy to capitalize on five quarters of double-digit growth in the category. The company recently created a new e-commerce business unit with Chief Digital Officer Yael Cosset taking the helm. By closing underperforming customer fulfillment centers ((CFC)) and reducing its workforce, Kroger (KR) aims to increase profitability in e-commerce by ~$400M albeit at a cost of ~$2.6B in impairment and related costs.

The decision to shutter its three CFCs was more a result of the disappointing return-on-investment than the company’s e-commerce strategy, the latter of which remains a pivotal part of Kroger’s (KR) growth objective.

Kroger’s (KR) push into a state-of-the-art CFC began in 2018 with a partnership between Kroger (KR) and UK-based Ocado Group plc (OTCPK:OCDGF) (OTCPK:OCDDY) to bring Ocado’s(OTCPK:OCDGF) (OTCPK:OCDDY) automated fulfillment capabilities to the U.S. and improve the efficiencies of Kroger’s (KR) growing home delivery service. Plans were formulated for 20 automated warehouses across the U.S.

The significant investment and time commitment meant the financial rewards could take years to realize. But in an attempt to compete with Walmart (WMT) and Amazon (AMZN), Kroger (KR) felt the investment was well worth the wait.

Kroger (KR) broke ground on the first CFC in Monroe, Ohio in June 2019 with an initial investment of $55 million. Two additional sites were selected that same year in Central Florida and the Mid-Atlantic region.

“This is a historic moment for our partnership. With construction starting for Kroger's first customer fulfillment center in the Cincinnati region, we are now well on the way to a nationwide revolution in how Kroger customers experience ecommerce," said Luke Jensen in 2019, CEO of Ocado Solutions. 

However, the Ocado-tooled (OTCPK:OCDGF) (OTCPK:OCDDY) facilities faced a myriad of issues impacting its efficiencies including location of the warehouses away from major metropolitan areas, lack of volume to cover the initial investment, and preference of U.S. consumers for speed over value, a dynamic that was unique over UK consumers where Ocado (OTCPK:OCDGF) (OTCPK:OCDDY) has been much more successful.

In 2023, Kroger (KR) paused the development of future Ocado-tooled CFCs stating that the existing facilities “did not meet the benchmarks we set for success.”

By September, Kroger (KR) CEO Ron Sargent conceded that the performance of the facilities was not consistent.

“Where we have seen strong demand in high-density areas, these facilities deliver better results than those facilities where density is lower, and customer adoption has been slower,” Sargent said on the company’s second quarter earnings call.

In the end, the decision to close the three CFCs may be a setback for Kroger’s automation vision, but does not end Kroger’s (KR) ambitions in digital grocery. With a leaner structure and a renewed focus on profitability, Kroger is betting that a smarter—not bigger—e-commerce strategy will give it a fighting chance against Walmart and Amazon.

data.title: Texas AG Paxton takes aim at Sanofi, Bristol over Plavix
data.description:
• Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton has set his sights on Sanofi (SNY) and Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY), accusing the drugmakers of marketing the blood thinner Plavix (clopidogrel) without letting patients and doctors know it is less effective for certain demographics.
  • In a lawsuit filed in Harrison County, Paxton accuses the drugmakers of falsely marketing Plavix, hiding information on its alleged subpar performance in certain ethnic groups, and making billions as a result.
  • He claims that Plavix, which has been available as a generic since 2012, has less of or no effect on individuals who identify as Black, East Asian, and Pacific Islander.
  • Paxton argues that the companies are in violation of the state's Health Care Program Fraud Prevention Act and consumer protection laws. 
  • Sanofi and Bristol have yet to return requests for comment from Seeking Alpha. 
  • Last month, the AG sued Kenvue (KVUE) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) over not providing a warning about a potential link between use of Tylenol in pregnancy and autism.

data.title: Cipher Mining announces pricing of $333M of additional senior secured notes
data.description:
• Cipher Mining (NASDAQ:CIFR) stated on Thursday that its wholly owned subsidiary, Cipher Compute LLC, has priced $333M worth of additional 7.125% senior secured notes due 2030 in a private offering pursuant to regulation S under the Securities Act.
  • Pursuant to the offering, the issuer will have $1.7B in 7.125% senior secured notes due 2030 outstanding.
  • The offering is expected to close on or about November 24, 2025.
  • The issuer intends to use the net proceeds from the offering to finance a portion of the construction costs associated with additional facilities within the Barber Lake Facility, near Colorado City, Texas.
  • The notes will be issued at 100.250%, plus pre-issuance accrued interest from and including November 13, 2025.

data.title: Post Holdings Non-GAAP EPS of $2.09 beats by $0.21, revenue of $2.25B in-line
data.description:
• Post Holdings press release (POST): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $2.09 beats by $0.21.
  • Revenue of $2.25B (+11.9% Y/Y) in-line.
  • Post management expects Adjusted EBITDA for fiscal year 2026 to be between $1,500-$1,540 million, inclusive of a partial year contribution from 8th Avenue's pasta business. Post management expects fiscal year 2026 capital expenditures to range between $350-$390 million, which includes Foodservice investment in continued cage-free egg facility expansion and the completion of the Norwalk, Iowa precooked egg facility expansion, for aggregate expenditures of $80-$90 million.

data.title: Ashford Hospitality sells three assets for $69.5M in gross proceeds; stock jumps
data.description:
Ashford Hospitality Trust (AHT) shares surged 20% in Thursday after-hours trading after the lodging REIT said it agreed to sell three assets for combined gross proceeds of $69.5M, most of which will be used to retire mortgage debt.

The sales -- comprising the Le Pavillon, New Orleans, a Tribute Portfolio Hotel, the Embassy Suites by Hilton Austin Arboretum, and the Embassy Suites by Hilton Houston Near the Galleria -- are expected to drive over $2M in annual cash flow improvement and $14.5M in future capital expenditure savings.

"Strategic asset sales will continue to play an important part in our plan to deleverage Ashford Trust while also improving cash flow and liquidity," Ashford President and CEO Stephen Zsigray said in a statement. "The majority of proceeds will be deployed immediately to retire mortgage debt, improving cash flow after debt service while eliminating sizeable future capital expenditure obligations."

The deal for the 226-room Le Pavillon is for $42.5M and expected to close in December 2025, while the agreement for Embassy Suites by Hilton Austin Arboretum and Embassy Suites is $27.0M and expected to be completed in January 2026. AHT noted there is no guarantee the sales will be completed on these terms or at all.

When adjusted for Ashford's (AHT) anticipated capex, the combined sale price represents a 2.2% cap rate on net operating income, or a multiple of 29.9 times hotel EBITDA for the 12 months ended Sept. 30, 2025. Excluding the expected capital spend, the combined sale price represents a 3.3% cap rate on NOI or a multiple of 19.5x hotel EBITDA for the 12 months ended Sept. 30, 2025.
